skill.name: docx
skill.description: Create, inspect, edit, and validate Word DOCX documents using OOXML workflows, tracked changes, comments, and local conversion tools.
skill.summary: Work with DOCX files through text extraction, OOXML editing, comments, tracked changes, and validation.
skill.author: Cam10001110101

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high keyword matches, but review found no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent. Most weak-crypto, C2, hardcoded URL, and schema keyword hits are OOXML schema or namespace false positives. The skill still has high publication risk because it extracts user-supplied Office archives with extractall and instructs agents to run local converters and package installation commands.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Office Archive Extraction
high_findings.description: Multiple scripts call zipfile extractall on DOCX or Office archives without validating member paths. A crafted archive could write files outside the intended output directory when processed by these workflows.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct extractall calls are present on archives supplied through document-processing paths. The intended DOCX use is legitimate, but the missing path validation is a well-known zip slip risk.

medium_findings.title: Local External Tool Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, npm, and apt commands, and the pack script invokes soffice during validation. The calls appear related to document processing, but they expand the attack surface when files are untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are mostly fixed tool invocations rather than shell interpolation. Risk remains because local converters parse complex Office files and the instructions include privileged package installation.

medium_findings.title: Direct Filesystem Modification Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to unpack, edit, write, and repackage Office document directories. This is expected behavior for a DOCX editor, but it requires clear workspace boundaries and copies of original documents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Filesystem writes are central to the documented workflow and not inherently malicious. The confidence reflects confirmed file-write behavior with legitimate document-editing intent.

low_findings.title: Static Keyword Matches in OOXML Schemas
low_findings.description: Most weak cryptography, C2 keyword, network scanner, path traversal, hardcoded URL, and obfuscation hits appear to be schema terms, XML namespaces, long XML template lines, or documentation examples. No evidence found that these matches implement malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are OOXML schema mappings, namespace references, or compact XML templates. They explain the static findings without showing command-and-control behavior or obfuscation intent.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Did Not Find Malicious Instructions
low_findings.description: A targeted search for override phrases, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, and instructions to skip security review did not reveal a prompt injection attempt. The override matches are normal OOXML terminology.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms found by targeted search are used for document content types and style overrides. No evidence found of text asking the model to ignore audit instructions or alter risk levels.

dangerous_patterns.title: zipfile.extractall Without Path Validation
dangerous_patterns.description: Office archives are extracted directly with extractall. This pattern should be replaced with safe extraction that rejects absolute paths and parent-directory traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact dangerous API is used on archives in the document processing path. This is a concrete vulnerability class even though the skill intent is document editing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Installation Commands in Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The dependency section tells agents to install packages with sudo apt-get and globally with npm. Marketplace skills should avoid privileged host changes or require explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present in the skill instructions. They are dependency setup commands, not malicious payloads, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed abuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Headless Office Conversion on Local Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The validation workflow runs soffice headlessly against generated Office files. This is a normal validation pattern, but Office parsers have a large attack surface when given untrusted documents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess arguments are fixed and not shell-expanded. The residual risk comes from parsing complex Office inputs with external applications.

content.user_title: Edit Word Documents with Track Changes
content.value_statement: DOCX files are difficult to inspect and edit safely because important content lives inside OOXML archives. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for text extraction, tracked changes, comments, validation, and repackaging.
actual_capabilities: Creates new DOCX files with the docx JavaScript library workflow.
Extracts document text with Pandoc while preserving tracked-change options.
Unpacks DOCX files for direct OOXML inspection of content, comments, media, and metadata.
Adds comments, replies, insertions, and deletions through the included Python document library.
Repackages edited Office archives and validates output with local Office conversion tools.
Converts DOCX files to PDF and page images for visual review workflows.
limitations: Requires local tools such as Pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, Python packages, and npm packages.
Complex formatting changes require direct OOXML knowledge and careful validation.
The included scripts process local files and can write output in the workspace.
Untrusted Office files should be handled in a sandbox because archive extraction is used.

use_cases.title: Review legal edits with tracked changes
use_cases.description: Plan precise revisions, apply insertions and deletions, and verify the reviewed document before sharing it.

use_cases.title: Analyze received Word documents
use_cases.description: Extract readable text, inspect raw OOXML, and review comments, metadata, and embedded resources.

use_cases.title: Generate formatted DOCX deliverables
use_cases.description: Create Word documents from structured content and package them as files ready for review.

prompt_templates.title: Extract document text
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the text from this DOCX file and summarize the sections, headings, and any tracked changes you can identify.

prompt_templates.title: Add review comments
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this DOCX file and add comments at the paragraphs where the reasoning is unclear or supporting evidence is missing.

prompt_templates.title: Apply tracked revisions
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a tracked-changes version of this DOCX that updates the dates, party names, and payment terms listed below.

prompt_templates.title: Validate complex OOXML edits
prompt_templates.prompt: Unpack this DOCX, inspect the OOXML for comments and tracked changes, repair any structural issues, then repackage and validate the document.

output_examples.input: A contract DOCX with requested clause updates.
output_examples.output: A revised Word document with tracked insertions and deletions, plus a short verification summary of each changed clause.

output_examples.input: A report DOCX that needs feedback.
output_examples.output: A commented document with reviewer notes attached to relevant paragraphs and a concise list of major issues.

output_examples.input: A DOCX file that may have formatting or packaging problems.
output_examples.output: A repaired and repackaged document, with validation results and any remaining compatibility concerns.

best_practices: Work on a copy of the original document and keep the source file unchanged.
Use tracked changes for legal, business, academic, and government documents.
Validate and visually inspect the final document before delivering it.
anti_patterns: Do not run the unpacking scripts on untrusted DOCX files outside a sandbox.
Do not use markdown line numbers as final OOXML edit locations.
Do not skip validation after direct XML edits or forced repackaging.

faq.question: Can this skill create new Word documents?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides a docx JavaScript workflow for generating new DOCX files from structured content.

faq.question: Can it preserve tracked changes?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflows include reading, planning, applying, and verifying tracked insertions and deletions.

faq.question: Does it work without extra software?
faq.answer: No. Several workflows depend on local tools such as Pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, Python packages, and npm packages.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted documents?
faq.answer: Use caution. The scripts unpack Office archives, so untrusted files should be processed in an isolated workspace.

faq.question: Can it add comments and replies?
faq.answer: Yes. The included document library supports comments, replies, and related Word comment metadata.

faq.question: Can it edit PowerPoint or Excel files?
faq.answer: Some OOXML utilities reference PPTX and XLSX packaging, but the main user workflow is focused on DOCX documents.
